summary: German-American landscape painter of the Hudson River School / Rocky Mountain School. Most commonly identified by The Rocky Mountains, Lander's Peak (1863) and The Last of the Buffalo. Often compared to Frederic Edwin Church. Built estate called Malkasten. Also associated with Luminism.

works.name: The Rocky Mountains, Lander's Peak (1863)
works.description: Bierstadt's masterpiece. Depicts Shoshone encampment with wigwams by a waterfall at Lander's Peak. Adapted from sketches on the Honey Road Survey Party. Exhibited in Boston for 25 cents in 1863. Compared to Church's Heart of the Andes. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Often compared to Frederic Edwin Church's Heart of the Andes due to similar central waterfall

The Rocky Mountains, Lander's Peak, is often compared to Frederic Edwin Church's Heart of the Andes

examples: exhibited for twenty five cents at a private venue in Boston in 1863 and depicted a feature sometimes known as Saber Tooth

clues.clue: Shoshone women camped by a Rocky Mountain lake at Lander's Peak; wigwams near a waterfall
examples: Shoshone women appear camped by a Rocky Mountain lake in a painting by this artist set at Lander's Peak
painted several wigwams at an Indian camp near a waterfall at Lander's Peak

works.name: The Last of the Buffalo (c. 1888)
works.description: Depicts a Native American on horseback attacking a buffalo. Rejected from the 1889 Paris Exposition as 'old-fashioned.' Wikipedia

works.name: General / Biographical

comprehensive_summary:
Albert Bierstadt was a German-American landscape painter associated with both the Hudson River School and the Rocky Mountain School. His masterpiece, The Rocky Mountains, Lander's Peak (1863), depicts a Shoshone encampment with wigwams by a waterfall, based on sketches from the Honey Road Survey Party. It was exhibited for 25 cents at a private venue in Boston and is frequently compared to Frederic Edwin Church's Heart of the Andes due to their similar central waterfalls.

The Last of the Buffalo (c. 1888) depicts a Native American on a rearing horse attacking a buffalo. It was rejected from the 1889 Paris Exposition Universelle as 'old-fashioned,' which marked a career lowlight after which Bierstadt mostly stopped painting. Among the Sierra Nevada, California (1868) shows elk dwarfed by mountains.

Bierstadt sold a painting to English railroad tycoon James McHenry for $25,000, with which he built his estate Malkasten. He was friends with philosopher Charles Sanders Peirce, enlisting him in invention schemes like transformable train cars. Congress refused to pay $80,000 for Capitol paintings, so he staged an unauthorized exhibition in the House Chamber. A shipwreck ended his Alaskan journey, inspiring The Wreck of the Ancon. His Roman Fish Market borrows from the Barberini Faun. He occasionally painted in the Luminist style and has a lake in Colorado named after him. Late-career tropical works include The Shore of the Turquoise Sea.
